note_data.title: Incomplete Records (AS6)
keyPoints: **Incomplete records** occur when a business does not maintain a full double-entry bookkeeping system
**Statement of Affairs** is prepared to find missing figures by listing assets and liabilities at opening and closing
**Capital comparison method** calculates profit by comparing opening capital with closing capital, adjusting for drawings and additional capital
**Margin** is gross profit expressed as a percentage of selling price: (Gross Profit ÷ Sales) × 100
**Mark-up** is gross profit expressed as a percentage of cost price: (Gross Profit ÷ Cost of Sales) × 100
**Sales ledger control account** is used to find missing sales, purchases or cash received from customers
**Bank summary** method reconstructs cash book from bank statements when cash records are incomplete
**Missing figures** are calculated using accounting equation: Assets - Liabilities = Capital
**Total debtors method** finds sales by analyzing opening debtors + sales - cash received = closing debtors

definitions.definition: A system where businesses do not maintain full double-entry bookkeeping, resulting in missing or inadequate accounting records
A statement similar to a balance sheet that lists assets and liabilities to determine capital when records are incomplete
Gross profit expressed as a percentage of sales revenue
Gross profit expressed as a percentage of cost of goods sold
A method of finding profit by comparing opening and closing capital after adjusting for drawings and additional capital introduced

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings clearly. Use control account format for debtors/creditors calculations.
Use proper headings and format. For Statement of Affairs, list assets and liabilities systematically.
Show the method used (e.g., capital comparison) and all adjustments made.

modelAnswer.question: Fatima's business has incomplete records. Opening capital was Rs.50,000. During the year she withdrew Rs.15,000 and introduced additional capital of Rs.8,000. Closing capital was Rs.65,000. Calculate the profit for the year.
modelAnswer.answer:
Capital comparison method:
Closing capital Rs.65,000
Less: Opening capital Rs.50,000
Increase in capital Rs.15,000
Add: Drawings Rs.15,000
Less: Additional capital Rs.8,000
Profit for the year Rs.22,000
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct adjustments for drawings and additional capital

commonMistakes: Never confuse margin with mark-up - margin uses sales as denominator, mark-up uses cost of sales
Students often forget to adjust for drawings and additional capital in capital comparison method
Never prepare Statement of Affairs like an Income Statement - it shows financial position, not profit
Students frequently add drawings instead of subtracting when calculating opening capital
Never use opening figures when closing figures are required in control accounts
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 2 Section B
Always show workings for margin/mark-up calculations
Use T-account format for sales/purchase ledger control accounts
Check that Statement of Affairs balances (total assets = total liabilities + capital)
formulas: Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings - Additional Capital
Sales = Opening Debtors + Credit Sales - Cash Received from Customers
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Statement of Affairs format showing Fixed Assets, Current Assets, Current Liabilities, Long-term Liabilities, and Capital calculation. Control account T-format showing opening balance, additions, deductions, and closing balance.
